Hash tables: two-table rehashing,渐进式 rehash
Lists: quicklist = list + ziplist hybrid
Sorted sets: skip list node layout, O(logN) ops
Small collections: intset and ziplist automatic promotion
Sets: hashtable vs intset vs listpack encoding decisions
Memory overhead comparison table across types
Practical: `OBJECT ENCODING` and `MEMORY USAGE` commands
Lessons: pick the right Redis type, memory adds up fast

ACLs: why POSIX.1e added finer-grained control
Capabilities: dropping root for least privilege (CAP_NET_RAW etc)
sudo: running-as-root vs running-as-user-with-elevated-rights

topic: CS 106 Intro: What happens when you compile and run `hello.c`? Tracing from preprocessor, through the C compiler, assembler, and linker to a running process on Linux.
audience: first-year CS students
style_hints: approachable, builds one phase at a time, light analogies
outline_hint: The mystery: you type `./hello` and 'Hello, world' — what happened?
Phase 1: preprocessor expands `#include <stdio.h>`
Phase 2: compiler translates C to assembly (x86-64) — show `objdump`
Phase 3: assembler turns `.s` → `.o` (machine code, relocations)
Phase 4: linker resolves `printf`, creates an ELF binary
Loading: the kernel maps the ELF, sets up the stack
The `main()` entry: `_start` → `__libc_start_main` → `main`
System call: `write(1, msg, len)` inside `printf`
File descriptors & the terminal: stdout connects to your tty
Summary: source → binary → process → syscall → screen
